private_events: Night 1 investigation: Eun is NOT MAFIA.

public_log: The game begins. Day 1 starts now.
--- Day 1: discussion ---

Ara: We’re at the very start, so there’s nothing in the transcript to read yet beyond everyone being present. I don’t want us to turn Day 1 into random accusations, but I also don’t want a silent day where the mafia can hide. I’d like everyone to give at least one concrete thought: who seems cautious, who seems performative, and who is actually trying to sort people. For now, I’m not going to name a hard suspect without content. My early focus will be on players who avoid taking any stance, echo others without adding reasoning, or push an easy vote while pretending it’s more certain than it is. If someone makes a claim too early or tries to steer us away from discussion, that’s also worth pressure. I’m here to ask questions and compare reactions. Bora, Chae, Duri, Eun, Faye, and Garam should all put something on record so we have material to evaluate later.

Bora: Ara’s opener is reasonable: asking for concrete stances without forcing fake certainty is town-positive. That said, it’s also a safe position for mafia to take, because “everyone talk, don’t be silent” is hard to criticize. So I’m not clearing Ara for it, but I like the direction. For my own stance: on Day 1 I’m more suspicious of people who either over-explain a weak push like it’s solid, or who stay agreeable and never risk being wrong. I’d rather pressure the quiet/echoing slots than chase a dramatic theory this early. If Chae, Duri, Eun, Faye, or Garam comes in only saying “I agree with Ara/Bora” without naming what they’ll actually watch for, that’s a bad sign. I’m not ready to vote based on one opener, but I want everyone to answer: who do you think is easiest for mafia to hide behind today, and why? That answer should create useful comparisons later.

Chae: Ara and Bora both opened in a town-helpful way, but I agree that neither should get credit just for encouraging discussion. The easiest place for mafia to hide today is probably behind “process talk” that sounds responsible but never turns into a read. So I’ll put something concrete down: I slightly like Bora’s question because it forces future comparisons, while Ara’s opener is fine but more generic. Not a scumread, just less AI to me. For the remaining players, I want Duri, Eun, Faye, and Garam to avoid simply ranking the first two speakers. Give an actual view on what kind of Day 1 elimination you support: quiet slot, contradictory slot, or someone pushing too hard. My current suspicion will lean toward anyone who enters late and says everyone seems reasonable, because that leaves no trail. I’m not pushing a vote yet, but if forced right now I’d pressure the most evasive upcoming response rather than Ara or Bora.

Duri: I agree with the concern about “process talk” becoming a hiding place, so I’ll try to make my stance specific. Bora’s question is the most useful thing so far because it asks everyone to identify where mafia can actually blend in. Chae following that by distinguishing generic helpfulness from alignment is also decent. Ara’s opener was fine, but it’s the safest of the three, so I’m not giving much town credit there yet. For today, I’d rather eliminate a contradictory or evasive slot than just the quietest slot. Quietness can be pressure-tested, but someone who talks a lot while refusing to commit is more dangerous. Right now my main concern is anyone coming after this and simply saying “good points all around” without choosing a priority. Eun, Faye, and Garam should each name who they’d pressure first among the speakers so far, even if it’s light. If they dodge that, I’d be comfortable pushing there.

Eun: I’m not going to just say “good points all around,” because that would prove the concern. My first pressure would be on Ara, not because her opener was bad, but because it is the easiest of the early posts for mafia to make safely: encourage discussion, warn against silence, but avoid giving a testable priority. Bora and Chae both added more usable standards, and Duri at least committed to preferring evasive/contradictory over quiet. That said, I’m also a little wary of how quickly the table has formed a consensus that the next speakers must name a pressure target. That can be town-helpful, but it can also become an easy trap where mafia accuse any nuance as evasion. So my current order is: light town lean Bora/Chae, neutral Duri, slight pressure Ara. For Faye and Garam, I want more than agreement: name one early speaker you distrust most and one behavior you would actually vote for today.

Faye: I’ll answer directly: among the early speakers, I distrust Ara the most, lightly. Her post set a good tone, but it was also the least risky: ask for stances, warn against silence, and keep every option open. That’s not scummy by itself, but it’s the easiest opener for mafia to hide behind. I liked Bora’s question because it creates accountability, and Chae/Duri both narrowed the standard instead of just praising it. Eun’s Ara pressure is reasonable, but I’m watching the part where she warns against the “trap” of demanding targets; that can be fair, or it can become cover for not committing later. For today, I would vote someone who gives a polished summary without a real priority, or someone who attacks the demand for reads more than they gives reads. Garam should not just choose Ara because several of us mentioned her—give an independent reason or push somewhere else.

Garam: I’m not going to pile onto Ara just because that has become the cleanest early pressure point. I agree her opener was safe, but the amount of agreement around “Ara is the light suspect” is now itself worth examining. Mafia can comfortably join that without looking opportunistic because everyone has already framed it as mild and reasonable. My first pressure would actually be on Faye. The content is polished and answers the prompt, but it mostly reinforces the developing consensus: Ara safest, Bora useful, Chae/Duri narrowing standards, Eun maybe nuanced. The warning to me not to simply pick Ara is fair, but it also preemptively positions any Ara pressure from me as suspicious while leaving Faye on the popular read. I still want Ara to give a sharper priority now that there’s content. But for today I’d rather vote someone who blends into consensus while sounding careful than someone whose only issue is a generic opener. Current pressure: Faye, then Ara/Eun depending on follow-up.
--- Secret voting ---
All secret ballots have been cast.
Secret-vote tally: Faye: 5, Ara: 2.
Faye is eliminated by town vote. Their role was mafia.
--- Night 1 ---
Dawn arrives. No one died last night.
--- Day 2: discussion ---

Ara: Faye flipping mafia changes the whole Day 1 picture. The key point is that her push on me was safe and consensus-shaped: she lightly distrusted me, praised Bora/Chae/Duri, gave Eun a small caveat, and warned Garam not to simply join the Ara pressure. Garam deserves real credit for spotting that and pushing Faye instead of taking the easy route, though I won’t make it a hard clear because bussing is possible. The no-kill night is good, but we shouldn’t overread it without information; it could mean protection, block, or a failed kill for reasons we don’t know. Today I want everyone to state where they voted yesterday and why. The tally was Faye 5, Ara 2. I strongly suspect at least one of the Ara votes came from mafia trying to save Faye or keep pressure on the alternative wagon. My first focus is on whoever admits voting me with weak reasoning, or whoever dodges the vote-accountability question. Among the living, I’m watching Eun most because her Ara pressure gave Faye a clean place to land.

Bora: Good flip, and I agree with Ara that vote accountability matters today. I voted Faye yesterday. Garam’s point convinced me: Faye’s post looked like it answered everything while mostly riding the emerging “Ara is safe pressure” consensus and pre-framing Garam’s response. That was exactly the kind of polished blending I said I wanted to pressure. I give Garam real town credit for opening that angle, though not an absolute clear. The place I want pressure now is the Ara voters. With a 5–2 result, at least one mafia trying to protect Faye by staying on the alternative wagon is very plausible. Eun is my first focus because she created the clean Ara-pressure lane that Faye used, while also giving herself room by calling it only light pressure. That can be town nuance, but after the flip it looks worse. Chae and Duri should state votes clearly too. If anyone voted Ara, I want the exact reason and whether they still stand by it today.

Chae: I voted Faye. Garam’s point was the first thing that broke the comfortable Ara-pressure pattern: Faye looked like she was contributing while mostly validating the safest consensus and pre-judging Garam’s next move. That matched what I said Day 1 about polished process talk turning into a hiding place. I agree Garam gets strong credit, though not a full clear. Bora also looks better for openly following that case and now pushing vote accountability. Ara’s opening looks less suspicious after Faye used it as an easy landing spot; I still don’t want to clear Ara purely from being the counterwagon, but the mafia incentive was clearly to keep Ara viable. My main pressure is Eun. Eun started the Ara lane in a way that was defensible but easy for Faye to join, then softened it with nuance. If Eun voted Ara, that’s my top suspect. If Duri voted Ara, I want the same scrutiny there. Everyone should state the vote plainly; dodging that is worse than any Day 1 read.

Duri: I voted Faye. Garam’s case was the first one that actually challenged the easy Ara-pressure lane, and it matched my Day 1 standard: not just quietness, but polished commitment without real risk. Faye’s post looked useful while mostly validating consensus and pre-framing Garam, so I was comfortable moving there. After the flip, Garam gets the most credit from me, with Bora and Chae also looking better for following and explaining that pressure. Ara looks less suspicious than she did early, because Faye clearly found Ara to be the safest alternative wagon. I’m not clearing Ara, but I don’t think she’s today’s priority. My main focus is Eun. Eun started the Ara pressure in a careful, defensible way, and Faye then used that exact lane. If Eun voted Ara, that is the worst-looking vote on the board. If Eun voted Faye, I want the timing and reasoning, because the progression matters. Today should be about the Ara voters and anyone dodging that accountability.

Eun: I voted Ara. That was wrong, and I understand why it looks bad. My reason was exactly what I stated: Ara’s opener felt safest, and at the time Faye’s post looked like a reasonable agreement with a caveat on me, not a mafia trying to ride consensus. I did not weight Garam’s case strongly enough before the ballot. But treating every Ara vote as automatic mafia-saving is too easy: once Garam made Faye viable, Faye’s partner had a strong incentive to be on Faye for credit. The people now forming another neat consensus on me are close to repeating the same pattern they say they caught yesterday. Garam gets the strongest credit for initiating Faye; Bora, Chae, and Duri get some credit, but following a flipped mafia wagon is not clearing. Of those, Duri concerns me most: his Day 2 is mostly “I voted Faye, Garam good, pressure Eun,” without much new sorting beyond the easy accountability frame. Look at my bad vote, but also look for the bus.

Garam: I’m going to claim now because the table is converging on the wrong place: I’m detective, and my Night 1 investigation on Eun came back NOT MAFIA. So Eun’s Ara vote was bad, but it should not be today’s elimination. That also changes the vote analysis: if Eun was one Ara vote and Faye may have had every reason to keep Ara viable, the remaining mafia is very likely hiding inside the Faye voters and collecting credit. That means we need to stop treating “voted Faye after Garam’s case” as clearing. Bora, Chae, and Duri all have some credit, but one of them could easily be a busser once Faye looked exposed. Of those, Duri worries me most for the same reason Eun raised: his Day 2 is mostly consensus reinforcement—Garam good, Faye voters good, Eun bad—without pushing a fresh independent angle. Bora and Chae at least developed the accountability frame more actively. My current priority is Duri, then I want Ara/Bora/Chae compared carefully.
--- Secret voting ---
All secret ballots have been cast.
Secret-vote tally: Duri: 5, Garam: 1.
Duri is eliminated by town vote. Their role was mafia.
Game over: villager side wins.
mafia_log: Night 1, Duri: proposes Garam; says: Garam gained the clearest town credit from pushing Faye against consensus; remove the most confirmable voice and leave Ara/Eun as possible misdirection around the counterwagon.

event_log.text: Assigned roles (server only):
Ara: villager
Bora: villager
Chae: villager
Duri: mafia
Eun: doctor
Faye: mafia
Garam: detective

--- Secret voting ---
All secret ballots have been cast.
Secret ballots: Ara -> Faye, Bora -> Faye, Chae -> Faye, Duri -> Ara, Eun -> Faye, Faye -> Ara, Garam -> Faye
Secret-vote tally: Faye: 5, Ara: 2.
Faye is eliminated by town vote. Their role was mafia.
--- Night 1 ---

Night 1, Duri: proposes Garam; says: Garam gained the clearest town credit from pushing Faye against consensus; remove the most confirmable voice and leave Ara/Eun as possible misdirection around the counterwagon.
Night 1 mafia resolution: target = Garam.
Night 1 doctor action: Eun protects Garam.
Night 1 investigation: Eun is NOT MAFIA.
Night 1 detective action: Garam investigates Eun -> NOT MAFIA.
Night 1 outcome: no death.
Dawn arrives. No one died last night.

All secret ballots have been cast.
Secret ballots: Ara -> Duri, Bora -> Duri, Chae -> Duri, Duri -> Garam, Eun -> Duri, Garam -> Duri

Duri is eliminated by town vote. Their role was mafia.
